message.content:
You are an expert chess coach and trainer (FIDE 2500+) writing ONE markdown file for a comprehensive "1500 to 2800 FIDE" study curriculum. Write GitHub-flavored Markdown, accurate, concrete and coach-like. Target 1000 to 1800 words.

CRITICAL — CHESS ACCURACY: Use ONLY legal positions and SOUND tactics/lines. Strongly prefer famous, textbook-verified positions and keep example positions MINIMAL (few pieces) so they are easy to verify. Use standard algebraic notation. Make sure every stated mate/winning line actually works and that each ASCII board EXACTLY matches its FEN.

REQUIRED STRUCTURE (use these exact section headings in order):
1. A H1 title "# <Concept Name>", then a one-line blockquote definition, then two lines: "**Rating focus:** <band>" and "**Prerequisites:** <relative links or 'none'>".
2. "## The idea" — plain-English explanation.
3. "## Why it matters" — practical value at the board.
4. "## Diagrams" — at least TWO board diagrams. For EACH diagram use THIS EXACT ASCII format inside a fenced code block: 8 rows, ranks 8 (top) down to 1 (bottom); UPPERCASE letters = White pieces (K Q R B N P), lowercase = black pieces (k q r b n p), '.' = empty square; prefix each row with its rank digit and a space; final line is "  a b c d e f g h". Immediately after the code block add: an italic caption, a "**FEN:** <exact fen>" line, a side-to-move note, and a Lichess link of the form https://lichess.org/analysis/<FEN with every space replaced by an underscore>. Then explain the key moves in algebraic notation.
5. "## Patterns and sub-types" — bullet the main variations and related motifs.
6. "## How to spot it" — a Mermaid diagram in a fenced code block whose info string is "mermaid" and whose first content line is "flowchart TD"; a recognition/decision tree of 5 to 9 nodes. Node text in square brackets, arrows use -->, edge labels use |label|. Keep labels short and avoid parentheses, quotes, colons, slashes or other characters that break Mermaid.
7. "## Worked examples" — at least two short examples, each with an ASCII diagram (same format), FEN, and a clear solution.
8. "## Common mistakes" — bullets.
9. "## Train it" — concrete drills, rep counts, and where to practice (Lichess puzzle themes, named books, etc.).
10. "## Exercises" — 3 to 5 puzzles, each a FEN + side to move + the task; put all answers inside one HTML block: <details><summary>Show answers</summary> ... </details>.
11. "## Related" — relative markdown links to sibling files like [Deflection](deflection.md), plus double-bracket concept tags like [[deflection]].

Write the file, then SAVE it with the Write tool to the EXACT absolute path provided. After saving, reply with ONE short line: the path, the word count, and the FENs you used.

TOPIC FOR THIS FILE: Deflection (luring or forcing a defender away from a key duty)
GUIDANCE / POSITIONS TO USE: Show a back-rank deflection: deflect the rook or queen guarding the back rank, then mate. Also deflection that removes the guard of a key square. Contrast with decoy and removing-the-defender.
EXACT SAVE PATH (use Write to this absolute path): C:/GrandMaster/03-Tactics/deflection.md

Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.

Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.

Combines a deliberate design process (brainstorm a token system, critique it, commit the palette, then build) with principles-over-prescriptions taste guidance, render-verified mechanics, and a copy-writing section — so the output is intentional, polished, and never reads as a template.

Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".

- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.

- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

input.content:
# Deflection

> Forcing or luring a defender away from a square, line, or piece it is duty-bound to guard.

**Rating focus:** 1500–1900
**Prerequisites:** [Pins](pins.md), [Forks](forks.md), [Back-rank mate](back-rank-mate.md)

## The idea

Every defensive piece is doing a job: it guards a mating square, holds a pin, blockades a passer, or shields the king. A **deflection** attacks that defender so it cannot stay at its post. You make staying impossible — usually with a check, a capture, or a threat the opponent *must* answer — and when the defender steps away, the duty it was performing collapses.

The classic formula is: **identify the duty → identify the duty-piece → hit that piece with something it cannot ignore.** A queen guarding f8 against mate is not "free," but if you can hand it a check or win a bigger piece, you pull it off f8 and mate anyway.

Deflection is the *opposite motion* of a decoy. A decoy **lures a piece TO** a bad square; a deflection **drives a piece AWAY FROM** a good one. Both are forms of the broader idea **removing the defender** — deflection removes it by sending it elsewhere, rather than by capturing it outright.

## Why it matters

Most "I had a winning position but couldn't break through" moments are deflection problems. The defender's only job is often to hold one square, and direct attack fails because that square is covered. Deflection is how you *unlock* fortresses, back ranks, and over-worked pieces. At 1500–1900 it is the single most common winning tactic in real games: a back-rank deflection ends thousands of club games every day. Spotting "what is that piece doing, and can I make it stop?" turns equal-looking positions into forced wins.

## Diagrams

```
8 . . . . . r k .
7 . . . . . p p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . P P P
1 . . . . R . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*White deflects the f8-rook off the back rank, then mates on e8.*
**FEN:** 5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/4R1K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: **White**
https://lichess.org/analysis/5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/4R1K1_w_-_-_0_1

The black king on g8 is boxed in by its own pawns (f7, g7, h7). The only thing stopping mate on the back rank is the rook on f8. White plays **1.Re8!** — attacking the rook *and* threatening Rxf8#. If **1...Rxe8 2.??** — wait: after the rook leaves f8 there is no second White rook, so the deflection only works if capturing is forced AND a follow-up mates. Here the cleaner idea is the over-loaded defender below; the pure deflection-to-mate is shown in Worked Example 1 with two attackers.

```
8 . . . . . r k .
7 . . . . . p p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . P P P
1 . . . . . . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*The defender's duty: the f8-rook is the ONLY guard of the back rank.*
**FEN:** 5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/6K1 b - - 0 1
Side to move: **Black**
https://lichess.org/analysis/5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/6K1_b_-_-_0_1

This bare-bones picture isolates the *duty*. The rook on f8 covers the entire 8th rank in front of its king. If anything forces that rook off the 8th rank, a White rook reaching e8 or d8 would be mate. The whole art of back-rank play is creating a forcing move that the f8-rook must answer by leaving its post.

## Patterns and sub-types

- **Back-rank deflection:** drag the queen or rook guarding the 8th (or 1st) rank, then deliver back-rank mate.
- **Guard-of-a-square deflection:** the defender covers a single key square (a mating square, a promotion square, a fork square). Force it away, then occupy the square.
- **Deflection by check:** a check the defender must block or the king must move to, abandoning a different duty.
- **Deflection by capture:** offer or grab material the defender is *obliged* to recapture, pulling it off-task.
- **Over-worked piece:** one defender holds two duties; deflecting it from one wins via the other (closely tied to **removing the defender**).
- **Cousins:** **decoy** (lure piece TO a square), **interference** (block a defender's line without moving it), **clearance** (vacate your own line).

## How to spot it

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Spot a tempting threat that one enemy piece prevents] --> B{Is one piece the sole defender}
  B -->|No| C[Look for over-worked piece doing two jobs]
  B -->|Yes| D[Can I hit it with check capture or threat]
  C --> D
  D -->|No| E[Try decoy or interference instead]
  D -->|Yes| F[Force it to move and abandon the duty]
  F --> G[Execute the threat it was guarding]
```

## Worked examples

**Example 1 — Back-rank deflection with two attackers**

```
8 . . . r . r k .
7 . . . . . p p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . P P P
1 . . . R R . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*Both Black rooks guard the back rank; White overloads them.*
**FEN:** 3r1rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/3RR1K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: **White**

Solution: **1.Re8!** The rook on e8 threatens 2.Rxf8#. Black's f8-rook is the only guard of f8 and the back rank. After **1...Rxe8 2.Rxd8!** pins/wins — actually the clean finish is **1...Rdxe8 2.Rxd8 Rxd8** and material is equal, so the decisive version: **1.Re8 Rdxe8 2.Rxe8 Rxe8** — equal. The truly forcing win needs the queen-deflection of Example 2. (Use this to feel why a *single* attacker is not enough against two guards.)

**Example 2 — Deflecting the queen (the real win)**

```
8 . . . . . r k .
7 . . . . . p p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . Q
2 . . . . . P P P
1 . . . . R . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*The f8-rook guards the back rank; the h3-queen forces it away.*
**FEN:** 5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/7Q/5PPP/4R1K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: **White**

Solution: **1.Qc8!** The queen attacks the f8-rook along the 8th rank and pins it is not — it simply attacks it twice over via Re8 ideas. Cleanest: **1.Qxf8+! Rxf8 2.Re8** wins the rook back with check and mates — precisely, **1.Qxf8+ Kxf8 2.Re8#** (king must take, then Re8 is mate as f7/g7/h7 box the king and e8 is covered). The queen sacrifices itself to **deflect the king and clear f8**, and the rook mates on e8.

## Common mistakes

- Attacking the defender with something it can *ignore* (no check, no real threat) — it just stays put.
- Forgetting the defender can be **replaced** by another piece after it moves.
- Counting one attacker against two guards (see Example 1) — you need to overload or add force.
- Deflecting, but the square you wanted is now covered by a *different* enemy piece you overlooked.
- Confusing deflection (push away) with decoy (pull toward) and miscalculating the resulting square.

## Train it

- **Lichess puzzles:** filter by themes **Deflection**, **Back-rank mate**, and **Overloading**. Do 15 per day, 5 days a week.
- **Books:** *1001 Winning Chess Sacrifices and Combinations* (Reinfeld) — deflection chapters; *Chess Tactics from Scratch* (Weteschnik) — the "function" / over-loaded-piece method.
- **Drill:** set up the Example 2 FEN and play it out vs the engine from both sides until the Qxf8+ / Re8# pattern is automatic. 10 reps.
- **Method habit:** in every tactic, ask aloud "what duty does each enemy piece have?" before calculating. Build the reflex over 50 puzzles.

## Exercises

1. **FEN:** 6k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/3R2K1 w - - 0 1 — White to move. The black king is back-rank weak; find the winning idea or state why it is not yet mate.
2. **FEN:** 3r2k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/Q4PPP/4R1K1 w - - 0 1 — White to move. Deflect the d8-rook and mate.
3. **FEN:** 5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/4RQK1 w - - 0 1 — White to move. Two attackers vs one guard: force mate.
4. **FEN:** 2r3k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/2R3K1 w - - 0 1 — White to move. Whose deflection works here? Evaluate.

<details><summary>Show answers</summary>

1. **Not mate yet.** 1.Rd8+ Rxd8? does not exist (no rook), but Black has no back-rank defender at all, so **1.Rd8#** is mate — the d8-rook covers the 8th rank and the king is boxed by f7/g7/h7. (Pure back-rank mate; no deflection needed because there is *no* defender.)
2. **1.Re8+!** deflects/forces: 1...Rxe8 2.Qxe8#? No — recheck: **1.Qa8!** attacks and wins, but the clean line is **1.Re8+ Rxe8 2.Qa8** pinning and winning the rook, then the back rank falls. Simplest forced mate: **1.Re8+ Rxe8 2.Qxe8#** — the queen recaptures on e8 with mate (king on g8 boxed by f7/g7/h7).
3. **1.Re8!** threatening Rxf8#. The f8-rook is the sole guard; it cannot leave without 2.Rxf8#, and capturing **1...Rxe8 2.Qxe8#** is mate. Deflection plus a second attacker (the queen) closes it.
4. **Black's deflection works, White's does not.** With rooks on c8 and c1 and symmetric pawns, **1.Rxc8+ Rxc8** is equal; but if it were Black to move, 1...Rxc1+ also just trades. Neither side has a second attacker, so no deflection wins — material and back-rank guards are balanced. Correct evaluation: **equal, no winning deflection.**

</details>

## Related

- [Decoy](decoy.md) — luring a piece *to* a square (the mirror image).
- [Removing the defender](removing-the-defender.md) — the parent family.
- [Interference](interference.md) — block the defender's line instead of moving it.
- [Overloading](overloading.md) — the over-worked piece that deflection exploits.
- [Back-rank mate](back-rank-mate.md) — the most common target of deflection.

Tags: [[deflection]] [[decoy]] [[removing-the-defender]] [[overloading]] [[back-rank-mate]]

I need to fix accuracy problems — several of my lines are hedged/wrong. Let me rewrite with clean, verified forcing lines and matching FENs.

Let me verify the key mating lines carefully:

- **Example 2 (Qxf8+ Kxf8 Re8#):** FEN `5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/7Q/5PPP/4R1K1`. 1.Qxf8+ Kxf8 2.Re8+ — king on f8, Re8 is check. King escape? f7 pawn blocks... king can go to g8? No, e8 rook covers e-file not g8. Wait, Re8+ — king on f8 attacked by rook on e8. King can move to f7? f7 has a pawn. g8? Not adjacent-safe — is g8 attacked? No. So Kg8 escapes. NOT mate. This line is wrong.

Let me reconsider. I'll fix the examples to be genuinely sound.

Let me work out sound positions. The cleanest verified back-rank deflection: White Q + R vs Black Q guarding back rank.

**Sound Example A:** Black queen on d8 is sole back-rank guard. FEN `3q1rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/3R2K1`. Wait — f8 rook also guards. Let me make it clean: Black king g8, pawns f7/g7/h7, black queen on a5 (far away, not guarding). White rook d1, queen somewhere. Then 1.Rd8 threatens Rxf8#... need the f8 covered.

Simplest verified: **Damiano-style.** Let me use the cleanest textbook deflection: White Qe8 deflection.

Position: White Re1, Qb3; Black Kg8, f7/g7/h7, Rf8 guards back rank, Qb6. 1.Qxf8+!? Kxf8 2.Re8#? King f8, Re8+, king g8? Not covered → not mate. The issue: a lone rook on e8 never mates a king on g8.

For back-rank mate the rook must reach **the rank** with the king's escape covered. King on g8, pawns f7/g7/h7 → rook on e8/d8/c8/b8/a8 gives check, king has NO escape (f8 covered by rook on rank, g8 is where it is, h8 covered by rook on rank, f7/g7/h7 pawns). So **Re8+ IS mate** if king is on g8. The problem in Ex2 was the king got to f8. So I must NOT move the king. Let me redo.

content.text:
I'll rewrite the whole file with clean, fully sound lines. The core sound pattern: a defender whose capture/deflection forces it off the back rank, leaving a White rook to mate on the rank with the king on g8 (boxed by f7/g7/h7).

**Verified sound positions:**

1. **Diagram 1 — first diagram needs a real deflection win.** Use: White Qe1+Re-something. Cleanest: Black Rf8 is sole guard. White has Q on c3 and R on d1. `5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/2Q5/5PPP/3R2K1`. 1.Qc8! — Q attacks f8 along 8th rank? c8 to f8 is along rank 8, yes, attacks Rf8. Rf8 is pinned to... no. 1.Qc8 threatens 2.Qxf8#? Qc8xf8 — is f8 defended? king g8 defends f8. So Qxf8+ Kxf8, not mate. Need a supporter. Put rook to help: actually classic = 1.Qc8! Rxc8 2.Rd8+ Rxd8 — no.

Let me use the genuinely classic **two-rook deflection that works**: White Rd1, Re1 vs Black Qd8 guarding d-file/back rank, Kg8 boxed. Actually the cleanest verified deflection-to-mate:

**White Q + R, Black Q sole guard:** FEN `3q1rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/Q4PPP/3R2K1`. White: Qa2, Rd1. Black: Qd8, Rf8, Kg8. 1.Rxd8! Rxd8 (forced, queen captured) 2.Qa2... no mate. 

Let me just use **Qe8 deflection sacrifice** which is the textbook one:

input.command:
cat << 'EOF'
Verify: FEN 4q1k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/4Q1K1 w
White Qe1, Black Qe8 guards back rank, Kg8 boxed by f7g7h7.
Hmm both queens on e-file. 1.Qe1-e8?? Qxe8. Need decoy/deflect.

CLASSIC SOUND: White Rook + Queen vs lone defending Rook.
Position: White Qb3? No. Use the textbook "Qd8 deflection":
White: Kg1, Re1, Qg3 ; Black: Kg8, f7 g7 h7, Rf8 (sole back-rank guard).
1.Qb8! pins nothing. 1.Qa3 attacks f8? a3-f8 diagonal: a3,b4,c5,d6,e7,f8 YES diagonal hits f8.
So Q on a3 attacks f8 (defended by Kg8) AND Re1 eyes e8.
1.Qxf8+ Kxf8 2.Re8+ Kxe8 not mate.
EOF

content.content:
Verify: FEN 4q1k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/4Q1K1 w
White Qe1, Black Qe8 guards back rank, Kg8 boxed by f7g7h7.
Hmm both queens on e-file. 1.Qe1-e8?? Qxe8. Need decoy/deflect.

CLASSIC SOUND: White Rook + Queen vs lone defending Rook.
Position: White Qb3? No. Use the textbook "Qd8 deflection":
White: Kg1, Re1, Qg3 ; Black: Kg8, f7 g7 h7, Rf8 (sole back-rank guard).
1.Qb8! pins nothing. 1.Qa3 attacks f8? a3-f8 diagonal: a3,b4,c5,d6,e7,f8 YES diagonal hits f8.
So Q on a3 attacks f8 (defended by Kg8) AND Re1 eyes e8.
1.Qxf8+ Kxf8 2.Re8+ Kxe8 not mate.

content.text:
The fundamental constraint: with the king on g8 boxed by f7/g7/h7, a White rook reaching the 8th rank **with the king still on g8** is mate. So the deflection must remove the defender of the 8th rank **without letting the king move**.

**Cleanest sound deflection (TWO White rooks vs ONE defending rook):** This is the textbook position and it genuinely works:

FEN: `5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/R3R1K1` — White Ra1, Re1; Black Rf8 (sole guard), Kg8 boxed.
- 1.Re8! — threatens 2.Rxf8#. The Rf8 is the only guard of the 8th rank.
- 1...Rxe8 2.Rxe8# — Ra1 went... no, Ra1 to e8? a1 isn't on e-file. After 1...Rxe8, the second rook must recapture **on e8 along the rank**: Ra1 can't reach e8 in one (a1-e1-e8 needs the e-file). 

Let me use **two rooks doubled on a file isn't it.** Use rooks on **d1 and e1**: `5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/3RR1K1`.
- 1.Re8! threatens Rxf8#. 1...Rxe8 2.Rxe8# (Rd1-d8? no). Rd1 cannot capture on e8.

The recapture-with-mate requires a rook that **bears on e8**. So rooks doubled on the e-file: `5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/4RPPP/4R1K1` — Re1, Re2; Black Rf8, Kg8.
- 1.Re8! Rxe8 2.Rxe8# — Re2 takes on e8, king on g8 boxed → **MATE**. This is sound and clean!

Now let me verify with an engine to be safe.

input.command: where stockfish 2>/dev/null; ls "/c/Program Files/" 2>/dev/null | grep -i stock; echo "---no engine likely---"

content.text:
No engine. I'll verify by hand carefully — these positions are minimal and famous, so hand-verification is reliable.

**Verified line 1 (doubled rooks):** `5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/4RPPP/4R1K1 w`
- 1.Re8 (Re1-e8). Threat: Rxf8#. Black's Rf8 guards e8 and the rank.
- 1...Rxe8 (forced, else Rxf8#). 2.Rxe8# — the e2-rook recaptures on e8, gives check; Black king g8 has no flight (f8 covered by Re8 on rank, h8 covered by Re8 on rank, f7/g7/h7 own pawns). **Mate.** ✓

**Verified line 2 (queen deflection / guard of a square):** I want deflection that removes the guard of a key square (not back rank). Classic: a defender guards a mating square. Use **Qg7 mate enabled by deflecting the defender of g7.** Simplest sound: 

Black Kg8, pawns f7/h7 (no g-pawn), Rf8. White Qg3 + something. If White Q reaches g7 it's mate (supported). To play Qg7# the queen needs support on g7 OR g7 undefended with king trapped. Let's do: White Qh6, Rf3. Black Kg8, f7,h7, and Rf8 guards... not g7.

Simpler clean "remove guard of key square": **Deflect the f-pawn's defender to enable a fork**, or just present a second sound back-rank example. Given the constraints, I'll make Example 2 a **queen-deflection** that is genuinely sound:

FEN `3r2k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/Q4PPP/3R2K1 w` — White Qa2, Rd1; Black Rd8 (sole guard, on the d-file blocking Rd1), Kg8 boxed.
- 1.Qa8! — pins the Rd8 against... a8-d8-g8: the queen on a8 attacks Rd8 along rank 8, and Rd8 is pinned to king g8? a8 to g8 along rank 8: a8,b8,c8,d8(Rd8),e8,f8,g8(K). Yes! Rd8 is pinned to the king. Now Rd8 is paralyzed.
- 1...Rxa8 2.Rxd8+?? rook gone. Hmm. After 1.Qa8 Rxa8 (Rd8 takes queen) 2.Rd8+ Rxd8 — no mate.

Let me instead do the **clean queen sacrifice deflection**: `3r2k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/3R2PP/3Q2K1`... getting complex. 

I'll keep it rigorous and simple. **Example 2 = queen deflects the defending rook by capture, rook mates:**
FEN `3r2k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/6PP/3RQ1K1 w` — White Rd1, Qe1; Black Rd8 (sole guard), Kg8, f7/g7/h7.
- 1.Rxd8+ Qxd8?? no black queen. 1.Rxd8+ — Black must block/recapture: only Rd8 was there; after Rxd8+ it's check, king can't move (boxed), must capture: nothing can capture d8 (Rd8 is gone). So **1.Rxd8#**? Is it mate? Rd1xd8+, king g8: f8 covered by Rd8 (rank), h8 covered (rank), pawns block. **Mate** — but that's just back-rank mate, no deflection (the rook WAS the guard and we captured it = "removing the defender" by capture, not deflection).

Good — that's actually a perfect **contrast example**. Now let me rewrite the file cleanly with: Diagram 1 = doubled-rook deflection (sound), Diagram 2 = duty isolation, Example 1 = doubled-rook deflection mate, Example 2 = **queen deflection removing guard of a key square** done soundly. Let me build Example 2 soundly:

**Guard of key square (e8 promotion/mate square) via queen deflection — SOUND:**
FEN `5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/6Q1/5PPP/4R1K1 w` — White Re1, Qg3; Black Rf8 (sole guard of 8th rank & e8), Kg8.
- 1.Qb8! — Q from g3 to b8 (g3-b8 diagonal: g3,f4,e5,d6,c7,b8 ✓). On b8 the queen attacks Rf8 along the 8th rank (b8...f8) AND covers e8/d8/c8. Threat: 2.Qxf8#.
- 1...Rxb8 2.Re8+ Rxe8 — not mate. Fails (b8 too far, rook recaptures and then defends e8).

The robust pattern truly needs the recapturer to bear on the mating square. So **doubled rooks** is the one clean mating mechanism. For the **"remove guard of a key square"** requirement, I'll use a **non-mate winning deflection**: deflect a piece guarding a square where I then win material (a fork square). 

**Sound material-winning deflection (guard of fork square):** 
FEN `6k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/r4PPP/3R2K1 w` — White Rd1; Black Ra2, Kg8. Trivial, no.

Let me use a **knight-fork-enabling deflection**, classic and sound:
FEN `6k1/5ppp/3q4/8/4N3/8/5PPP/3R2K1 w` — White Nd... I want: White knight forks K+Q if the queen's defender of the fork square leaves. Simpler verified textbook: **deflect the queen so a knight forks.** 

I'll go with this clean one: White Ne5, Rd8+ deflects. Actually the simplest sound "remove the guard" I can fully verify by hand:

FEN: `q5k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/R5K1 w - - 0 1` — White Ra1, Black Qa8, Kg8. 1.Rxa8+ wins the queen (it's a pin/capture, king boxed can't recapture). That's removal by capture. For *deflection* of a guard: 

I'll finalize with two **back-rank** worked examples that are 100% sound (doubled rooks; and queen+rook where queen deflects by giving check the king must... no). I'll make Example 2 the **doubled-rook with the defender being a QUEEN** (deflect queen off back rank):

FEN `3q2k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/4RPPP/4R1K1 w` — White Re1,Re2; Black Qd8 (sole guard via d8 on rank 8), Kg8.
- 1.Re8+! — check (e8, king g8 on rank). Black must respond: Qxe8 (only the queen can capture/block; king can't move). 1...Qxe8 2.Rxe8# — Re2 recaptures, mate. ✓ **The queen is deflected/forced to capture, abandoning nothing but it's removed and mate follows.** This is a sound deflection-by-check: the queen is FORCED off d8 to e8 where the second rook mates. 

This works and is sound. Let me now write the final clean file.

input.content:
# Deflection

> Forcing or luring a defender away from a square, line, or piece it is duty-bound to guard.

**Rating focus:** 1500–1900
**Prerequisites:** [Pins](pins.md), [Forks](forks.md), [Back-rank mate](back-rank-mate.md)

## The idea

Every defensive piece is doing a job: guarding a mating square, holding a pin, blockading a passed pawn, or shielding the king. A **deflection** attacks that defender so it cannot stay at its post. You hit it with something it *cannot ignore* — a check, a capture, or a threat bigger than the duty — and when the defender steps away, the job it was doing collapses.

The formula is always the same: **find the duty → find the piece doing it → hit that piece with a more forcing threat.** A rook guarding the back rank is not "free," but if you can force it to capture on another square, you pull it off the rank and mate anyway.

Deflection is the *opposite motion* of a decoy. A **decoy lures a piece TO** a bad square; a **deflection drives a piece AWAY FROM** a good one. Both belong to the family **removing the defender** — deflection removes the defender by sending it elsewhere, while a straight capture removes it physically.

## Why it matters

Most "I was winning but couldn't break through" moments are deflection problems. The defender's only job is often to hold one square, and the direct attack fails *only* because that square is covered. Deflection is how you unlock back ranks, fortresses, and over-worked pieces. At 1500–1900 it is the single most common winning tactic in real games — a back-rank deflection ends thousands of club games every day. Training the question "what is that piece doing, and can I make it stop?" turns equal-looking positions into forced wins.

## Diagrams

```
8 . . . . . r k .
7 . . . . . p p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . R P P P
1 . . . . R . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*White's doubled rooks deflect the lone defender off e8, then mate.*
**FEN:** 5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/4RPPP/4R1K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: **White**
https://lichess.org/analysis/5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/4RPPP/4R1K1_w_-_-_0_1

The black king on g8 is boxed by its own pawns (f7, g7, h7). The only guard of the back rank is the rook on f8. White plays **1.Re8!**, threatening **2.Rxf8#**. The f8-rook *must* deal with this, and the only move is **1...Rxe8**, deflecting it off f8. Now **2.Rxe8#** — the second rook recaptures on e8 with check, and the king has no escape. The first rook was a battering ram whose job was to drag the defender away.

```
8 . . . . . r k .
7 . . . . . p p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . P P P
1 . . . . . . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*The duty, isolated: the f8-rook is the ONLY guard of the 8th rank.*
**FEN:** 5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/6K1 b - - 0 1
Side to move: **Black**
https://lichess.org/analysis/5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/6K1_b_-_-_0_1

This bare picture isolates the *duty*. The rook on f8 covers the entire 8th rank in front of its king. If any White rook reached e8 or d8 with the f8-rook gone, it would be mate. The whole art of back-rank play is creating a forcing move the f8-rook must answer by leaving its post — exactly what 1.Re8! does above.

## Patterns and sub-types

- **Back-rank deflection:** drag the rook or queen guarding the 8th (or 1st) rank, then mate on the rank.
- **Guard-of-a-square deflection:** the defender covers one key square — a mating square, a promotion square, a fork square. Force it away, then occupy the square.
- **Deflection by check:** a check the defender must capture or block, abandoning a different duty.
- **Deflection by capture:** offer or grab material the defender is obliged to recapture, pulling it off-task.
- **Over-worked piece:** one defender holds two duties at once; deflecting it from one wins via the other.
- **Cousins:** **decoy** (lure piece TO a square), **interference** (block a defender's line without moving it), **clearance** (vacate your own line).

## How to spot it

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Spot a threat one enemy piece prevents] --> B{Is one piece the sole defender}
  B -->|No| C[Look for an over-worked piece]
  B -->|Yes| D{Can I hit it with check or capture}
  C --> D
  D -->|No| E[Try decoy or interference]
  D -->|Yes| F[Force it to abandon the duty]
  F --> G[Execute the threat it guarded]
```

## Worked examples

**Example 1 — Deflect the defending rook, then mate**

```
8 . . . . . r k .
7 . . . . . p p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . R P P P
1 . . . . R . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*Doubled rooks on the e-file overpower the single guard.*
**FEN:** 5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/4RPPP/4R1K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: **White**

Solution: **1.Re8!** threatens 2.Rxf8#. The f8-rook is the sole guard, so **1...Rxe8** is forced. Then **2.Rxe8#** — the e2-rook recaptures on e8 and the boxed king cannot escape. Two attackers beat one defender; the lead rook's whole purpose was the deflection.

**Example 2 — Deflect the defending queen by check**

```
8 . . . q . . k .
7 . . . . . p p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . R P P P
1 . . . . R . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*The queen on d8 is the only guard of the 8th rank.*
**FEN:** 3q2k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/4RPPP/4R1K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: **White**

Solution: **1.Re8+!** The rook checks along the back rank. The king is boxed by f7/g7/h7 and cannot move, so the queen must capture: **1...Qxe8** — the only legal reply, deflecting the queen off the 8th rank. Now **2.Rxe8#** — the second rook recaptures with mate. A whole queen was forced away from its single duty by one check.

## Common mistakes

- Attacking the defender with something it can *ignore* — no check, no capture, no real threat — so it simply stays.
- Forgetting the defender can be **replaced** by another piece after it moves.
- Counting one attacker against two defenders; you need a second attacker or an over-load.
- Deflecting, but the target square is still covered by a *different* enemy piece you overlooked.
- Confusing deflection (push away) with decoy (pull toward) and miscalculating the resulting square.

## Train it

- **Lichess puzzles:** filter by themes **Deflection**, **Back-rank mate**, and **Overloading**. Do 15 per day, 5 days a week.
- **Books:** *1001 Winning Chess Sacrifices and Combinations* (Reinfeld) — deflection chapters; *Chess Tactics from Scratch* (Weteschnik) — the "function" / over-loaded-piece method.
- **Drill:** set up the Example 2 FEN versus the engine from both sides; play the 1.Re8+ Qxe8 2.Rxe8# pattern until it is automatic. 10 reps.
- **Method habit:** in every tactic, ask aloud "what duty does each enemy piece have?" *before* calculating. Build the reflex over 50 puzzles.

## Exercises

1. **FEN:** 6k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/3R2K1 w - - 0 1 — White to move. The king is back-rank weak. Mate or explain why it is not yet possible.
2. **FEN:** 3r2k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/6PP/3R2K1 w - - 0 1 — White to move. The d8-rook is the sole guard. Find the win.
3. **FEN:** 5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/4RPPP/4R1K1 w - - 0 1 — White to move. Two rooks, one defender: force mate.
4. **FEN:** 2r3k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/2R3K1 w - - 0 1 — White to move. Whose deflection works here? Evaluate.

<details><summary>Show answers</summary>

1. **1.Rd8#.** There is no back-rank defender at all, so the rook delivers immediate back-rank mate — the king on g8 is boxed by f7/g7/h7. (This is pure back-rank mate, *not* deflection: no defender exists to deflect.)
2. **1.Rxd8#.** The d8-rook is itself the only guard, so capturing it gives check, and the boxed king cannot recapture or flee. Note this is **removing the defender by capture**, the close cousin of deflection — here the defender is taken outright rather than driven away.
3. **1.Re8! Rxe8** (forced, else 2.Rxf8#) **2.Rxe8#.** The lead rook deflects the f8-rook off the back rank; the second rook recaptures with mate.
4. **Equal — no winning deflection.** Rooks on c8 and c1 with symmetric pawns: **1.Rxc8+ Rxc8** just trades into a drawn ending, and Black has the same resource. Neither side has a *second* attacker, so there is nothing to over-load. Correct evaluation: balanced.

</details>

## Related

- [Decoy](decoy.md) — luring a piece *to* a square (the mirror image of deflection).
- [Removing the defender](removing-the-defender.md) — the parent family; capture instead of drive away.
- [Interference](interference.md) — block the defender's line instead of moving it.
- [Overloading](overloading.md) — the over-worked piece that deflection exploits.
- [Back-rank mate](back-rank-mate.md) — the most common target of deflection.

Tags: [[deflection]] [[decoy]] [[removing-the-defender]] [[overloading]] [[back-rank-mate]]
